Just a thought.

author: @driverjamescopeland
text:
Pelton - "Let's turn the fluid 180° to put more energy into the turbine."

Tesla - "Let's turn the air thousands of degrees to put more energy into the turbine."

author: @driverjamescopeland
text:
Definitely interesting... but I'd have left the splitter out of the foils (just made them nearly U-shaped), and opened the inner side slightly more. This way, you still get a near 180° turn, but also creating a slightly lower pressure zone to the inside.

There's still going to be a significant amount of inefficiency, due to centrifugal force trying to push the air back out.

text: Soooo... just as a thought experiment... let's assume you would print those pelton elements separately and put these on little axles that stick out the ground plate just like the blades do, so that they can swivel a bit. In my head the oncoming wind, when hitting the one bucket more than the other, would then turn the pelton element so that the less filled bucket would get more wind. Or am I thinking wrong?

author: @glencahalin4786
text:
My thoughts on an air Pelton turbine is to not go the route of 2 distinct pieces, for the vanes, but to combine them into an Asymmetric curved arrow-head shape. The outer side being roughly the shape of the current straight vanes, but angled in slightly to end in the middle of the rear in front. For the rear, the outer edge being longer than the inner edge, which will subsequently mean a difference in rate of curve.

Admittedly part of my thinking, for doing that, is then blades become a more solid/robust one piece component, and easier to assemble if scale up or printed as separate parts eg on a smaller 3D printer

author: @stefanwaterbouwer8139
text:
Pelton turbines in hydropower application generally require quite a large head or pressure to operate in optimal range of efficiency. Perhaps testing the Pelton wheel with compressed air jets could be a more controlled way to determine its efficiency? 
Also, chambers Pelton hydropower wheels are housed in are designed with precise geometry to make sure water leaving the wheel doesn't hit it again, slowing it down. That may also be an interesting path to explore regarding airpower applications.

In any case, great video and love seeing you back in the saddle with Patti by your side with the beautiful framed photo!

author: @happygardener28
text: A question please. 3:52 if you made your ring with a flange to concentrate the flow of the wind could  it be directed to another smaller turbine before being released from the channel, or would that second turbine be inefficient due to the energy expended in the first?

replies.author: @grendel1960a
replies.text: this is what was done in the early days of steam with the triple expansion engine, each cylinder using lower pressure than the previous, obviously with diminishing returns at cylinder 3.

replies.author: @happygardener28
replies.text: @grendel1960a But with the steam engines you were also dealing with the loss of pressure as the water cools. I'm not very conversant about engineering as my field is accounting. But as new wind turbines are developed I'm trying to learn as much as I need to evaluate investment opportunities. Robert's tinkering is very informative.

replies.author: @grendel1960a
replies.text: @happygardener28 Thats ok, I too was curious on one of the other wind turbine designs whether you could use the air flowing out to turn another turbine, and actually 3d printed a second turbine to rotate outside the first as an experiment, I found it did pick up some rotation, but not quite as much as I had been expecting, ie if the initial turbine is converting 80% of the energy in the wind, then you wont get the remaning 20% from a second, so its a law of diminishing returns. and I was never ccertain if the second turbine slowed the first.

replies.author: @happygardener28
replies.text: @grendel1960a Logic implies it might seriously reduce the energy collected in the following turbine as the second turbine could, not will just potentially, impede the outflow from the original. Depending on the design and method of collection at the second turbine in conjunction with the outflow of the first, far too many factors for my lack of knowledge to consider.
